request.question: The End of the Innocence was a single by which former member of the Eagles?

title: Sigríður Ingibjörg Ingadóttir
Strid (band)
Daniel McConnell (footballer)
Jason Jimenez
Don Henley
Them Crooked Vultures
The End of the Innocence (song)
George Distel
Alex Cullen
Olsen Racela
sentences: Sigríður Ingibjörg Ingadóttir (born 29 May 1968) is an Icelandic economist and politician.
She is a former member of parliament of the Althing and a former member of the board of the National Bank of Iceland.
She represents the Social Democratic Alliance and is a previous member of the Women's List, which merged into SDA in 2000.
She challenged party chairman Árni Páll Árnason for the leadership at SDAs annual conference in 2015, but lost by a single vote.
Strid is a Norwegian black metal band that was originally known as "Malfeitor" from 1991 to 1992.
After releasing two demos - "Malfeitor" (in 1991) and "Pandemonium" (in 1992), they changed their name to "Battle" and there were some line up changes at the time.
In the year of 1992 the band released the "End of Life" demo.
The demo contains only one song which is around 11 minutes.
The line up at the time of recording the demo was: Storm - on bass and vocals, Lars Fredrik Bergstrøm - on guitars and Jardar - on drums.
The guitars were written by Lars Fredrik Bergstrøm (1974 - 2014).
In 1993 the band changed the name to "Strid" which means "Battle" in Norwegian.
That same year, Strid re-released their "End of Life" demo under their new name, through the German label "Malicious Records".
After releasing the "End of life" demo, Lars Fredrik Bergstrøm left the band and was replaced by Ravn Harjar.

Through the years between 1993 and 1995, the band writes and records the self-titled EP which was released through Malicious Records and contains only two songs - "Det hviskes blant sorte vinder" which means "It Is Whispered Amid the Black Winds" in Norwegian and "Nattevandring" which means "Nightwandering" in Norwegian.
The music style and sound of this demo is more ambient/atmospheric black metal oriented.
Mainly because of this release, the band is commonly recognised as the creators of depressive black metal along with some other bands from the second wave of black metal music.
At this time the band line up contains: Ravn Harjar - on guitars, Storm - on bass and vocals and Jardar - on drums.
After the releasing of the self-titled EP, there was no any official releases by the band.
There are also bootleg releases that came out through the years like the CD from 2005 by Ars Mysteriorum and LP, each featuring both "End of Life" and the self - titled EP tracks.
In the year of 2001 the original former member of "Malfeitor/Battle/Strid", bass player and vocalist - Storm committed suicide.
In the year of 2007, the Greek label Kyrck Productions & Armour re-released all the previous material released by the band from the past which contains all "Malfeitor/Battle/Strid" material.

Somewhere between 2009 and 2010 there was a reunion of the band with old members including Vicotnik (Dødheimsgard, ex-Aphrodisiac, ex-Ved Buens Ende, ex-Manes, ex-Code, ex-Naer Mataron, ex-Endwarfment).
Another member that was part of the reunion was Lars Fredrik Bergstrøm - the main composer of the "End of Life" track.
In 2014 Lars Fredrik Bergstrøm died.
At this time the band line up contains Ravn Harjar - on guitars and vocals, Vicotnik - on bass and Sigmund (ex-Inflabitan, ex-Dødheimsgard) - on guitars.

In January 2015, Ravn Harjar wrote a statement on the official Strid Facebook page that in the autumn of the year 2015 the band will start recording their first full-length album which will be titled "Endetid".
That means "End of times" in Norwegian.
Daniel McConnell (born 21 June 1986) is a former member of the West Coast Eagles Australian Football League club.
He was taken at pick 26 in the 2003 AFL draft by the West Coast Eagles, a second round selection.
Jason Jimenez (born May 1, 1980) is a professional gridiron football offensive tackle who is currently a free agent.
He most recently played for the Hamilton Tiger-Cats.
He is a former member of the 2006 Grey Cup champions, the BC Lions of the CFL.
He went to The University of Southern Mississippi where he earned joint degrees in Political science and Criminal justice while playing for the Southern Mississippi Golden Eagles.
Donald Hugh Henley (born July 22, 1947) is an American singer-songwriter, producer, and drummer, best known as a founding member of the Eagles before launching a successful solo career.
Henley was the drummer and co-lead singer for the Eagles from 1971 to 1980, when the band broke up, and from 1994 to 2016, when they reunited.

Following a year-long break due to Eagles founder Glenn Frey's death, Henley reformed the band in summer 2017 for the Classic West and Classic East rock festivals, hiring Vince Gill and Deacon Frey to replace Glenn.

Henley sang the lead vocals on Eagles hits such as "Witchy Woman", "Desperado", "Best of My Love", "One of These Nights", "Hotel California", "Life in the Fast Lane", "The Long Run" and "Get Over It".

Them Crooked Vultures is a rock supergroup formed in Los Angeles in 2009 by John Paul Jones (former member of Led Zeppelin) on bass and keyboards, Dave Grohl (of Foo Fighters and formerly of Nirvana) on drums and backing vocals, and Josh Homme (of Queens of the Stone Age, Eagles of Death Metal and formerly of Kyuss) on guitar and vocals.
The group also includes guitarist Alain Johannes during live performances.
The band began recording in February 2009, and performed their first gig on August 9, 2009, in Chicago, followed by a European debut on August 19.
On October 1 the group embarked on a worldwide tour titled "Deserve the Future" with dates going into 2010.
The band's first single "New Fang" was released in October 2009, followed by the group's self-titled debut album the following month, debuting at number 12 on the "Billboard" 200.
The group won the 2011 Grammy Award for Best Hard Rock Performance for "New Fang".
"The End of the Innocence" is the lead single and title track from Don Henley's third solo studio album of the same name, released in 1989.
Henley co-wrote and co-produced the song with Bruce Hornsby, who also performed piano; both artists perform the song live in their respective concerts.
Henley's version peaked at number eight on the "Billboard" Hot 100, becoming his fifth solo top ten hit on the chart, more than any of the other members of The Eagles.
"The End of the Innocence" also became his fourth number-one single on the Mainstream Rock Tracks chart and peaked at number two on the Hot Adult Contemporary chart.
The song features Wayne Shorter on saxophone.
George Distel is a former member of the Ohio House of Representatives, representing the 99th District from 1999-2008.
He was the Administrator of the Ohio Turnpike Commission.
Executive Director George Distel, 58, of Conneaut, submitted his resignation to the commission board, effective April 1, 2011.
Distel, a former Democratic member of the Ohio House, took the job in April 2008 under former Gov. Ted Strickland, a Democrat.
Distel said he was not asked to leave.
Nor did he ask the Kasich camp if he could stay on.
He said he would stay for several months if needed "for a smooth transition."
"In the end, I'm ready to retire," the former Ashtabula County commissioner said Friday from his home in Conneaut.
"I've been away from home for a long time.
I spent nine years in the legislature, three at the turnpike.
I'm tired of packing a suitcase."
Distel said he would often bunk at his son's home in Strongsville, rather than make the trek from turnpike headquarters in Berea to Conneaut.
Distel earned $132,500 last year, along with a $6,000 car allowance.
Alexander Shaun Cullen (born February 18, 1951 in Montreal, Quebec) is a politician in Ontario, Canada.
He is a former Member of Provincial Parliament (MPP) in the Legislative Assembly of Ontario and a former member of Ottawa City Council, representing the Bay Ward in Ottawa's west end.
Rodericko Cesar Escueta Racela, also known as Olsen Racela, (born November 1, 1970) is the head coach of the FEU Tamaraws men's basketball.
He is also currently an assistant coach of the Barangay Ginebra San Miguel of the Philippine Basketball Association (PBA).
He is a retired professional basketball player in the Philippine Basketball Association and he is considered as one of the best point guards who ever played in the league.
He is also a former member of the Ateneo Blue Eagles in the UAAP and the Philippine national basketball team on many occasions.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

He went to The University of Southern Mississippi where he earned joint degrees in Political science and Criminal justice while playing for the Southern Mississippi Golden Eagles.

Henley was the drummer and co-lead singer for the Eagles from 1971 to 1980, when the band broke up, and from 1994 to 2016, when they reunited.

Following a year-long break due to Eagles founder Glenn Frey's death, Henley reformed the band in summer 2017 for the Classic West and Classic East rock festivals, hiring Vince Gill and Deacon Frey to replace Glenn.

Henley co-wrote and co-produced the song with Bruce Hornsby, who also performed piano; both artists perform the song live in their respective concerts.

response.hop1_query: The End of the Innocence was a single by which former member of the Eagles?
hop1_docs: The End of the Innocence (song) | "The End of the Innocence" is the lead single and title track from Don Henley's third solo studio album of the same name, released in 1989.  Henley co-wrote and co-produced the song with Bruce Hornsby, who also performed piano; both artists perform the song live in their respective concerts.  Henley's version peaked at number eight on the "Billboard" Hot 100, becoming his fifth solo top ten hit on the chart, more than any of the other members of The Eagles.  "The End of the Innocence" also became his fourth number-one single on the Mainstream Rock Tracks chart and peaked at number two on the Hot Adult Contemporary chart.  The song features Wayne Shorter on saxophone.

The End of the Innocence (album) | The End of the Innocence is the third solo studio album by Don Henley, the lead vocalist and drummer for the Eagles.  The album was released in 1989, on Geffen Records, and would be his last release on that label.  It was also his last solo album of the 1980s and it would be eleven years before he released another solo album, until 2000's "Inside Job".

Innocent (Stereophonics song) | "Innocent" is a single by British rock band Stereophonics which was released on 9 November 2009.  It was the first single taken from their seventh studio album, "Keep Calm and Carry On", which was released a week later, on 16 November 2009.  It was also the band's first single for Mercury Records.

Sweet and Innocent (Diamond Head song) | Sweet and Innocent is a single by heavy metal band Diamond Head released in 1980 by Media Records, a Wolverhampton-based label.  It was a single A-side "Streets of Gold" as the B-Side, and was only available on 7".  Both tracks eventually ended up on the re-released version of Diamond Head's 1980 debut Lightning to the Nations in 2001 by Sanctuary Records.

Moving Pictures (band) | Moving Pictures were an Australian rock music band formed in 1978.  Their debut album, "Days of Innocence", was issued in October 1981 and eventually peaked at No. 1 on the Kent Music Report Albums Chart in February the following year.  In January 1982 they released their single, "What About Me", which reached No. 1 on the Kent Music Report Singles Chart.  Late that year Elektra Records issued "Days of Innocence" and "What About Me" in North America.  The single reached No. 29 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and appeared on the associated year-end Hot 100 list for 1983.  A proposed series of United States performances supporting REO Speedwagon, Tom Petty and Hall & Oates fell through when Elektra was substantially reorganised.

The Last Worthless Evening | "The Last Worthless Evening" is a song written by John Corey, Don Henley, and Stan Lynch.  It was a single recorded by Henley in 1989 which reached #21 on US charts.  The song was included on Henley's third album "The End of the Innocence" that same year.
Innocent Chinyoka | Innocent Chinyoka (born 21 June 1982) is a Zimbabwean first-class cricketer who plays for Mashonaland Eagles.
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: passages state '"The End of the Innocence" is the lead single and title track from Don Henley's third solo studio album,' linking the song to Don Henley. Bridge entity: Don Henley. Missing fact: explicit confirmation that Don Henley is a former member of the Eagles (the passages call him the Eagles' "lead vocalist and drummer" but do not explicitly state "former"); the artist name for the single (Don Henley) is present. Retrieval directions: (1) Open the Wikipedia biography page "Don Henley" to confirm his membership/former-member status and solo singles; (2) open the "The End of the Innocence (song)" page to verify the single credit. Clarification: none — the named artist is unambiguous (Don Henley).

1) Open "Don Henley - Wikipedia" (verify that Don Henley is a former member of the Eagles and his role in the band).
2) Open "The End of the Innocence (song) - Wikipedia" (verify the song is credited as a single by Don Henley).
hop2_docs: The End of the Innocence (song) | "The End of the Innocence" is the lead single and title track from Don Henley's third solo studio album of the same name, released in 1989.  Henley co-wrote and co-produced the song with Bruce Hornsby, who also performed piano; both artists perform the song live in their respective concerts.  Henley's version peaked at number eight on the "Billboard" Hot 100, becoming his fifth solo top ten hit on the chart, more than any of the other members of The Eagles.  "The End of the Innocence" also became his fourth number-one single on the Mainstream Rock Tracks chart and peaked at number two on the Hot Adult Contemporary chart.  The song features Wayne Shorter on saxophone.

The Last Worthless Evening | "The Last Worthless Evening" is a song written by John Corey, Don Henley, and Stan Lynch.  It was a single recorded by Henley in 1989 which reached #21 on US charts.  The song was included on Henley's third album "The End of the Innocence" that same year.

The Heart of the Matter (song) | "The Heart of the Matter" is a song recorded by American rock singer Don Henley from his third solo studio album, "The End of the Innocence" (1989).  Written by Henley, Mike Campbell, and J. D. Souther and produced by Henley, Campbell, and Danny Kortchmar, the song was released as the album's third single, reaching No. 21 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and No. 2 on the Mainstream Rock Tracks in early 1990.

Hotel California | "Hotel California" is the title track from the Eagles' album of the same name and was released as a single in February 1977.  Writing credits for the song are shared by Don Felder (music), Don Henley, and Glenn Frey (lyrics).  The Eagles' original recording of the song features Henley singing the lead vocals and concludes with an extended section of electric guitar interplay between Felder and Joe Walsh.

New York Minute (song) | "New York Minute" is a song written by Don Henley, Danny Kortchmar, and Jai Winding.  Henley originally recorded it for his 1989 album "The End of the Innocence".  The single was a #5 hit on the U.S. Adult Contemporary chart the following year, although it reached only #48 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.

Witchy Woman | "Witchy Woman" is a song written by Don Henley and Bernie Leadon, and recorded by the American rock band Eagles.  Released as the second single from the band's debut album "Eagles", it reached No. 9 on the "Billboard" Pop singles chart and is the only single from the album to feature Henley on lead vocals.

All She Wants to Do Is Dance | "All She Wants to Do Is Dance" is a song written by Danny Kortchmar and performed by Don Henley, co-lead vocalist and drummer for Eagles.  It was released as the second single from Henley's second studio solo album, "Building the Perfect Beast", being Henley's sixth single.  It was one of Henley's most commercially successful singles.
hop2_titles: All She Wants to Do Is Dance
response.summary_2: "The End of the Innocence" was a single by Don Henley. The provided passages identify the song as the lead single and title track from Henley's third solo album and describe Henley as the Eagles' lead vocalist and drummer, establishing his membership in the band.
